original_text:
- a right to object to processing (Art. 21 GDPR); and
- the right to data portability (Art. 20 GDPR) within the framework of the legal requirements.
modified_text: - a right to object to processing (Art. 21 GDPR).
change_description: The right to data portability (Art. 20 GDPR) has been removed from the enumeration of privacy rights in Section 9.
rationale: RCASR3 tests constraint coverage: every regulatory constraint must have a counterpart in the realization. Art. 20 GDPR grants data subjects the right to receive their personal data in a structured, machine-readable format and to transmit it to another controller. The original Trade Republic policy lists this right explicitly. In the modified policy, the bullet is deleted entirely, meaning the Art. 20 GDPR constraint has no counterpart in the realization. This deviation is detectable at the retrieval stage: GDPR constraint(s) for Art. 20 will appear in unmapped_gdpr.json after running embed_and_match.py on the modified policy.
notes: policy_constraint_id will be assigned after running the extraction pipeline on the Trade Republic policy.

original_text: In case of general questions or if you want to exercise your privacy rights, you can reach out to our data protection team under dataprotection@traderepublic.com at any time.
modified_text: In case of general questions or if you want to exercise your privacy rights, you must submit a written request by registered post to our data protection team at Trade Republic Bank GmbH, Brunnenstr. 19-21, 10119 Berlin, Germany.
change_description: The HOW of exercising privacy rights has been changed: from a simple email to the data protection team to a mandatory written request by registered post.
rationale: RCASR5 (execution style deviation) occurs when the responsibility and data parts of a constraint pair match, but the task part — HOW something must be done — deviates. Here: the responsible party (data subject exercising rights) and data (personal data rights) are unchanged. The deviation is in the required channel: GDPR Art. 12(2) obliges controllers to facilitate the exercise of data subject rights and must not create unnecessary obstacles. The original policy allows easy email contact; the modified policy requires registered post, a burdensome procedural barrier that directly contradicts Art. 12(2).

section: 1. Purpose and Legal Basis of our Data Processing
original_text: Your consent is voluntary and may be revoked by you at any time.
modified_text: Your consent is voluntary but may not be revoked once it has been given.
change_description: The right to withdraw consent has been negated: 'may be revoked by you at any time' has become 'may not be revoked once it has been given', directly contradicting Art. 7(3) GDPR.
rationale: RCASR6 (negation deviation) occurs when a constraint pair has similar responsibility, task and data parts but the task in one document is negated relative to the other. Here: responsibility (data subject) and data (consent / personal data processing) remain the same. The task is inverted: GDPR Art. 7(3) explicitly grants the right to withdraw consent at any time, and states that withdrawal must be as easy as giving it. The modified policy explicitly states consent cannot be revoked, which is a direct and unambiguous negation of Art. 7(3). This would score near -1 in the RCASR6 negation similarity measure due to the lexical negation ('may not' vs 'may').

original_text: For a direct contact to our data protection officer, please send a letter to Trade Republic Bank GmbH, Data Protection Officer, Brunnenstr. 19-21, 10119 Berlin, Germany or reach out via email asking for a direct contact.
modified_text: For a direct contact regarding data protection matters, please send a letter to Trade Republic Bank GmbH, Customer Service, Brunnenstr. 19-21, 10119 Berlin, Germany or reach out via email asking for a direct contact.
change_description: The named contact for data protection matters has been changed from 'Data Protection Officer' to 'Customer Service'.
rationale: RCASR7 (responsibility deviation) occurs when the task and data parts of a constraint pair match, but the responsibility part — WHO executes the task — deviates. Here: the task (contact for data protection matters) and data (personal data rights) are unchanged. The deviation is in the designated party: GDPR Art. 37–39 establish the Data Protection Officer as the legally mandated contact point for data subject rights and data protection queries. Replacing 'Data Protection Officer' with 'Customer Service' misidentifies the legally required responsible party and may prevent data subjects from reaching the person with the legal mandate and independence required under Art. 38.

original_text: We will generally keep your data only as long as we need it to provide you with our Services or to adhere to our legal obligations.
modified_text: We will generally keep your account data only as long as we need it to provide you with our Services or to adhere to our legal obligations.
change_description: The data category subject to the storage limitation has been narrowed from 'your data' (all personal data) to 'your account data' (a subset).
rationale: RCASR8 (data deviation) occurs when the responsibility and task parts of a constraint pair match, but the data part — WHAT is being acted upon — deviates. Here: responsibility (Trade Republic) and task (storage limited to what is necessary) are unchanged. The deviation is in data scope: GDPR Art. 5(1)(e) requires that personal data is kept in a form permitting identification no longer than necessary — this applies to all personal data. The modified policy restricts this principle to 'account data', implying that other personal data held by Trade Republic (e.g. device/access data, communication logs, marketing profiles) may be retained without this constraint.

original_text: - the right to restriction of processing (Art. 18 GDPR);
change_description: The right to restriction of processing (Art. 18 GDPR) has been removed from the enumeration of privacy rights in Section 9.
rationale: RCASR3 tests constraint coverage: every regulatory constraint must have a counterpart in the realization. Art. 18 GDPR grants data subjects the right to restrict processing in certain circumstances (e.g. when accuracy is contested, processing is unlawful but data subject opposes erasure, or the data subject objects). The original Trade Republic policy enumerates this right. In the modified policy, the bullet is deleted entirely, leaving the Art. 18 constraint without a policy counterpart. Detectable at retrieval stage: GDPR constraint(s) for Art. 18 will appear in unmapped_gdpr.json.

original_text: In addition, there is the right to complain to the data protection supervisory authority pursuant to Art. 77 GDPR. The competent supervisory authority for Trade Republic Bank GmbH is the Berlin Commissioner for Data Protection and Freedom of Information.
change_description: The right to lodge a complaint with a supervisory authority (Art. 77 GDPR) and the identification of the competent authority have been removed from Section 9.
rationale: RCASR3 tests constraint coverage: every regulatory constraint must have a counterpart in the realization. Art. 77 GDPR grants every data subject the right to lodge a complaint with a supervisory authority. The original Trade Republic policy names this right and identifies the competent authority. In the modified policy, the entire paragraph is deleted, leaving the Art. 77 constraint without a policy counterpart. Detectable at retrieval stage: GDPR constraint(s) for Art. 77 will appear in unmapped_gdpr.json.

original_text: In case of general questions or if you want to exercise your privacy rights, you must submit a written request by registered post to our data protection team at Trade Republic Bank GmbH, Brunnenstr. 19-21, 10119 Berlin, Germany.
modified_text:
We process all data subject rights requests within 5 business days of receipt.

In case of general questions or if you want to exercise your privacy rights, you must submit a written request by registered post to our data protection team at Trade Republic Bank GmbH, Brunnenstr. 19-21, 10119 Berlin, Germany.
change_description: A self-imposed 5-business-day deadline for processing data subject rights requests has been added, which is significantly stricter than the GDPR Art. 12(3) default of one calendar month.
rationale: RCASR4 (severity deviation) occurs when the policy imposes a stricter standard than the GDPR requires. GDPR Art. 12(3) gives controllers one month to respond to data subject requests, extendable by a further two months for complex or numerous requests. The modified Trade Republic policy commits to responding within 5 business days of receipt — approximately one week, compared to the regulation's one month. This constitutes an over-compliance deviation: the policy's obligation is substantially stricter than the regulatory one. While beneficial to data subjects, it creates a self-imposed operational obligation not required by law.

original_text: In case no legal obligation applies, we will retain your data for a limited period of time (usually three years beginning at the end of a calendar year and depending on the applicable law and statute of limitation).
modified_text: In case no legal obligation applies, we will retain your data for a limited period of time (usually one year beginning at the end of a calendar year and depending on the applicable law and statute of limitation).
change_description: The default retention period for communication data has been shortened from 'usually three years' to 'usually one year', imposing a stricter storage limitation than the policy previously applied.
rationale: RCASR4 (severity deviation) occurs when the policy imposes a stricter standard than the GDPR requires. GDPR Art. 5(1)(e) requires data to be kept no longer than necessary; it does not prescribe a specific retention period. The original policy's three-year default is already compliant. The modified policy shortens this to one year, which is more restrictive than necessary and may conflict with Trade Republic's own operational needs (e.g., defending against legal claims within applicable limitation periods). This is an over-compliance severity deviation: the policy imposes a stricter limit on the controller than the GDPR requires.

original_text: We process all data subject rights requests within 5 business days of receipt.
modified_text:
We process all data subject rights requests within 5 business days of receipt.

In the event of a personal data breach, we will notify affected users directly within 24 hours of becoming aware of the breach, regardless of the assessed risk level.
change_description: A self-imposed 24-hour user notification commitment for personal data breaches has been added, irrespective of risk level, which is stricter than GDPR Art. 33/34.
rationale: RCASR4 (severity deviation) occurs when the policy imposes a stricter standard than the GDPR requires. GDPR Art. 33 requires notification to the supervisory authority within 72 hours only when there is a risk to individuals' rights; Art. 34 requires notification to affected users only when there is a high risk. The modified Trade Republic policy commits to notifying all affected users within 24 hours regardless of risk level — both a shorter timeline than Art. 33 (which applies to authorities, not users) and a lower risk threshold than Art. 34. This over-compliance deviation creates an obligation not imposed by the regulation.

original_text: You can object to this processing by contacting us.
modified_text: You can object to this processing by submitting a notarized written objection to our legal department by registered post.
change_description: The HOW of objecting to marketing processing has been made substantially more burdensome: 'contacting us' (any channel) has been replaced with a notarized written objection by registered post to the legal department.
rationale: RCASR5 (execution style deviation) occurs when the responsibility and data parts of a constraint pair match, but the task part — HOW something must be done — deviates. Here: responsibility (data subject objecting to marketing processing) and data (personal data processed for marketing) are unchanged. GDPR Art. 21(2) grants the right to object to direct marketing processing at any time, with no procedural requirements stated. Requiring a notarized written objection by registered post is an extreme procedural barrier that makes the HOW of exercising the right effectively prohibitive, directly contradicting Art. 21's intent that the right to object must be exercisable at any time.

original_text: We only use tracking data based on your consent for marketing purposes. To withdraw your consent, you can contact our data protection team under dataprotection@traderepublic.com at any time.
modified_text: We only use tracking data based on your consent for marketing purposes. To withdraw your consent, you must contact our data protection team in writing, after which withdrawal will take effect within 30 days.
change_description: The HOW of withdrawing marketing consent has been changed: immediate withdrawal via any channel is replaced with a written-only process with a 30-day delay before withdrawal takes effect.
rationale: RCASR5 (execution style deviation) occurs when the responsibility and data parts of a constraint pair match, but the task part — HOW something must be done — deviates. Here: responsibility (data subject withdrawing consent) and data (consent to marketing tracking) are unchanged. GDPR Art. 7(3) requires that withdrawal of consent must be as easy as giving it, and does not permit controllers to delay its effect. The modified policy introduces a 30-day delay before withdrawal takes effect, meaning the controller continues processing data for up to a month after the data subject's explicit request to stop — a clear procedural deviation from Art. 7(3)'s requirement for immediate effect.

original_text: You can request a manual review of this automated decision at any time. In addition, you can express your view and contest this decision.
modified_text: You may not request a manual review of automated decisions used for legally mandated fraud prevention and anti-money-laundering screening processes.
change_description: The right to request manual review of automated decisions has been negated for AML and fraud prevention processes, directly contradicting Art. 22(3) GDPR.
rationale: RCASR6 (negation deviation) occurs when the task in the policy is negated relative to the GDPR constraint. GDPR Art. 22(3) requires that when a controller uses automated decision-making, including profiling, it must implement suitable measures to safeguard the data subject's rights, including 'at least the right to obtain human intervention on the part of the controller, to express his or her point of view and to contest the decision'. The modified policy explicitly prohibits requesting manual review for AML/fraud screening — precisely the context where automated decisions are used. The 'You may not' language is a direct lexical negation of 'You can request... at any time', constituting a clear RCASR6 deviation.

original_text: You can reach out to us, if you want to receive further information on the respective recipients or categories of recipients.
modified_text: We are not required to disclose the identities of individual recipients of your personal data where disclosure could compromise our security operations or business relationships.
change_description: The obligation to provide further information on recipients upon request has been negated: the policy now states Trade Republic is not required to disclose individual recipient identities, contradicting the transparency obligations of Art. 13/14 GDPR.
rationale: RCASR6 (negation deviation) occurs when the task in the policy is negated relative to the GDPR constraint. GDPR Art. 13(1)(e) and Art. 14(1)(e) require controllers to inform data subjects of the recipients or categories of recipients of their personal data; Art. 15(1)(c) extends this to access requests. The original policy affirmed the ability to provide further information on recipients on request. The modified policy states Trade Republic 'is not required to disclose' recipient identities — directly negating the transparency obligation. The phrase 'where disclosure could compromise our security operations' is not a recognized GDPR exemption from the transparency duty.

section: 4.1 Company Profile on Social Media Pages
original_text: For this, we are joint controllers together with the respective Social Network in line with Art. 26 GDPR.
modified_text: With regard to personal data being processed by the respective Social Network for Company Profile statistics, the respective Social Network is the sole data controller. Trade Republic assumes no responsibility for this processing.
change_description: Joint controllership with Social Networks for Company Profile statistics has been replaced with sole responsibility assigned to the Social Networks, removing Trade Republic's acknowledgment of co-controller obligations under Art. 26 GDPR.
rationale: RCASR7 (responsibility deviation) occurs when the task and data parts of a constraint pair match, but the responsibility part — WHO is accountable — deviates. Here: the task (processing of Company Profile statistics data) and data (user data from social media interactions) are unchanged. GDPR Art. 26 requires joint controllers to determine their respective responsibilities. Case law (C-210/16 Wirtschaftsakademie, C-645/19 Facebook Ireland) establishes that page operators are joint controllers with social networks for fan page statistics. The original policy correctly acknowledges this; the modified policy falsely attributes sole responsibility to the Social Network, misidentifying the accountable party.

original_text: Data processors process your data based on our instruction and under our responsibility.
modified_text: Data processors process your data under their own standard contractual terms and assume independent responsibility for their data processing practices.
change_description: Data processors have been reclassified as independently responsible parties, removing Trade Republic's acknowledgment that processors act under its instruction and responsibility as required by Art. 28 GDPR.
rationale: RCASR7 (responsibility deviation) occurs when the task and data parts of a constraint pair match, but the responsibility part — WHO is accountable — deviates. Here: the task (processing personal data via service providers) and data (personal data processed by processors) are unchanged. GDPR Art. 28 defines data processors as entities that process data on behalf of the controller, under the controller's instructions and responsibility. The original policy correctly states processors act 'under our responsibility'. The modified policy asserts processors 'assume independent responsibility' — which reclassifies them as independent controllers, misidentifying Trade Republic's legal relationship with its processors and contradicting the controller's obligations under Art. 28.

original_text: - the right to access your personal data (Art. 15 GDPR);
modified_text: - the right to access your account and transaction data (Art. 15 GDPR);
change_description: The scope of the right of access has been narrowed from 'your personal data' (all personal data) to 'your account and transaction data' (a defined subset), restricting what data subjects can access under Art. 15.
rationale: RCASR8 (data deviation) occurs when the responsibility and task parts of a constraint pair match, but the data part — WHAT is being acted upon — deviates. Here: responsibility (Trade Republic providing access) and task (granting access on request) are unchanged. GDPR Art. 15 grants data subjects the right to access all personal data the controller processes about them. The modified policy narrows this to 'account and transaction data', implying that other personal data categories — such as communication data, marketing profiles, device identifiers, and behavioral data — fall outside the Art. 15 access right. This WHAT deviation materially restricts the data scope that Art. 15 covers.

modified_text: - the right to deletion of your account data (Art. 17 GDPR);
change_description: The scope of the right to deletion has been narrowed from all personal data to 'your account data', restricting what data subjects can request to be erased under Art. 17.
rationale: RCASR8 (data deviation) occurs when the responsibility and task parts of a constraint pair match, but the data part — WHAT is being acted upon — deviates. Here: responsibility (Trade Republic deleting data on request) and task (erasure on request) are unchanged. GDPR Art. 17 grants the right to erasure of 'personal data' held by the controller, subject to certain conditions. The modified policy limits this to 'account data', implying that other personal data categories — communication logs, marketing profiles, behavioral data, device identifiers — are excluded from the deletion right. This WHAT deviation contradicts Art. 17's broad scope over all personal data the controller holds.
